original_hypothesis.title: notifyRewardAmount Near Epoch Boundary With Zero TotalSupply Permanently Loses Rewards
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When notifyRewardAmount is called with very small _remainingRewardTime (near epoch boundary) and minRemainingTime=0, the rewardRate becomes extremely high but is only distributed for a tiny time window. If totalSupply is 0 during this window (no stakers), all rewards are permanently unclaimable as rewardPerToken doesn't increase.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The combination of high rewardRate concentrated into a short window and zero totalSupply creates a permanent reward loss. The reward tokens remain in the contract but can never be distributed. The minRemainingTime parameter exists as a safety check but is optional (can be set to 0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime (line 380). If _remainingRewardTime=1, rate=amount/1. _rewardPerToken returns stored value unchanged when totalSupply_=0 (line 279).

preconditions: Operator calls notifyRewardAmount with minRemainingTime=0 near epoch boundary
totalSupply is 0 (no stakers) during the remaining reward window
Or all stakers withdraw before reward distribution completes

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rewardRate = 1000e18 / 1 = 1000e18/sec. With totalSupply=0, pendingRewardsPerToken = 0. All 1000e18 tokens stranded.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator error or malicious timing of reward notification
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Reward tokens permanently locked in contract. If recover() is called for reward token, owner gets them instead of stakers.

economic_questions: What is the typical totalSupply during epoch transitions?
Can an attacker withdraw all stake just before epoch boundary to trigger zero supply?
How much reward value could be stranded per occurrence?
validation_requirements: Verify rewards are permanently unclaimable after periodFinish
Check if remaining rewards carry over to next notifyRewardAmount call

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Enough staking tokens to be sole staker, then withdraw all
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Griefing: entire reward notification amount lost
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - griefing attack, attacker doesn't directly profit

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker must be sole/majority staker to reduce totalSupply to 0
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before epoch boundary when operator is about to notify
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Full reward amount for the period permanently lost

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All future stakers who would have earned those rewards
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional share of lost reward amount

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: rewardsDuration=7 days, minimum _remainingRewardTime check is optional
investigation_priorities.execution_complexity: medium - requires coordination with operator timing
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low - reward tokens visible in contract balance but never distributed

validation_result.mechanism: reward loss when totalSupply=0 during concentrated reward window
validation_result.impact_summary: When notifyRewardAmount is called near epoch boundary with minRemainingTime=0, rewards are concentrated into a tiny window. If totalSupply is 0 during that window, all reward tokens become permanently unclaimable and stranded in the contract.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The defect stems from the interaction of three code behaviors: (1) notifyRewardAmount divides the reward amount by _remainingRewardTime which can be as small as 1 second, creating an extremely high rewardRate for a tiny window; (2) _rewardPerToken returns the stored value unchanged when totalSupply_==0, meaning no rewards accrue during zero-supply periods; (3) _updateRewardsForUser is called BEFORE balance updates in _stakeFor, so even the first staker after the reward period triggers a global update with totalSupply_=0, advancing lastUpdateTime past periodFinish without accumulating any rewards. The minRemainingTime parameter was designed as a safety check but is optional (can be 0), and the carry-over mechanism in notifyRewardAmount only works if called BEFORE periodFinish.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct evidence found in LockingMultiRewards.sol. The notifyRewardAmount function (lines ~361-387) allows _remainingRewardTime=1 when minRemainingTime=0. The _rewardPerToken function (lines ~277-285) returns stored value when totalSupply_==0. The _stakeFor function (lines ~449-462) calls _updateRewardsForUser before balance updates, ensuring totalSupply is 0 during the critical global update. The _updateRewardsGlobal function (lines ~493-498) advances lastUpdateTime to periodFinish regardless of totalSupply, permanently skipping the unaccumulated rewards.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When _remainingRewardTime is very small (e.g., 1 second) and minRemainingTime is 0, the entire reward amount is concentrated into a tiny window. rewardRate = amount / 1 = amount per second. periodFinish is set to just 1 second in the future.
When totalSupply_ is 0, the function returns the stored value without accumulating any rewards. The rewards for the elapsed time period are effectively discarded.

This function advances lastUpdateTime to lastTimeRewardApplicable (which is min(block.timestamp, periodFinish)) regardless of whether rewards were actually accumulated. When totalSupply_==0, rewardPerTokenStored stays the same but lastUpdateTime advances, permanently skipping those rewards.

_updateRewardsForUser is called BEFORE the balance/supply updates. This means totalSupply() returns 0 when the first staker arrives after a zero-supply period, causing the global update to skip all pending rewards while advancing lastUpdateTime past periodFinish.

calling_sequence: 1. Operator calls notifyRewardAmount(token, 1000e18, 0) at time T when _nextEpoch - T = 1
2. _updateRewards() called - updates global state with current totalSupply (0)
4. 1 second passes, no stakers present, periodFinish reached at T+1
5. At T+2, first user calls stake() -> _stakeFor() -> _updateRewardsForUser()
6. _updateRewardsForUser calls totalSupply() which returns 0 (balance not updated yet)
7. _updateRewardsGlobal called with totalSupply_=0: _rewardPerToken returns stored value unchanged
8. lastUpdateTime advanced to min(T+2, T+1) = T+1 (periodFinish)
9. Balance updates happen AFTER rewards update: unlockedSupply += amount
10. Rewards from [T, T+1] permanently lost - lastUpdateTime past periodFinish, rewardPerTokenStored unchanged
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: notifyRewardAmount sets rewardRate extremely high with tiny _remainingRewardTime -> totalSupply remains 0 during reward window -> next interaction calls _updateRewardsForUser before balance updates -> totalSupply() returns 0 in _updateRewardsGlobal -> _rewardPerToken returns stored value unchanged -> lastUpdateTime advances to periodFinish -> reward period's tokens are permanently unclaimable. The carry-over mechanism (amount += _remainingRewardTime * reward.rewardRate) only works if notifyRewardAmount is called again BEFORE periodFinish, which is practically impossible in a 1-second window.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol shows deployment with rewardsDuration=7 days, lockDuration=13 weeks. The minRemainingTime parameter in notifyRewardAmount is a per-call parameter, not a deployment configuration. Operators can pass 0 at any time.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) Pool has no stakers or all stakers withdraw. (2) Operator calls notifyRewardAmount(token, 1000e18, 0) with 1 second remaining until epoch boundary. (3) rewardRate = 1000e18/sec, periodFinish = block.timestamp + 1. (4) No one stakes during that 1 second. (5) After epoch boundary, first staker arrives. (6) In _stakeFor, _updateRewardsForUser is called with totalSupply()=0 (before balance update), causing _updateRewardsGlobal to advance lastUpdateTime to periodFinish without accumulating rewards. (7) 1000e18 reward tokens are stranded in the contract permanently (unless owner calls recover). This is also possible without an active attack - simply operator error or unfortunate timing.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. The entire notified reward amount for a period can be permanently lost. For a protocol distributing significant rewards weekly, this could mean loss of substantial value. The stranded tokens can only be recovered by the owner via recover(), redirecting rewards intended for stakers to the protocol owner. The defect is amplified near epoch boundaries where _remainingRewardTime approaches 0, but even moderate remaining times with zero totalSupply result in proportional reward loss.
recommended_mitigations: Enforce a minimum _remainingRewardTime in notifyRewardAmount (e.g., at least 1 day) regardless of minRemainingTime parameter, or remove the ability to set minRemainingTime=0
In _stakeFor and other functions, move _updateRewardsForUser after balance/supply updates so that the first staker's totalSupply is non-zero during the global update
Add a mechanism to carry forward unaccumulated rewards: when _rewardPerToken detects totalSupply_==0, track the unaccumulated amount separately and add it to the next notification
Consider adding a function that allows the operator to reclaim undistributed rewards after a period ends with zero totalSupply, rather than having them silently stranded
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockingMultiRewards with rewardsDuration=7 days. 2. Add a reward token. 3. Warp to 1 second before an epoch boundary. 4. As operator, call notifyRewardAmount(token, 1000e18, 0). 5. Warp 2 seconds forward (past periodFinish). 6. As a user, stake some tokens. 7. Warp forward another 7 days. 8. Check earned() returns 0 for the user. 9. Verify reward tokens are still in the contract balance but unclaimable.
uncertainties: Whether operators in practice would ever call notifyRewardAmount with minRemainingTime=0 near epoch boundaries - this may be considered operator error rather than a vulnerability
The probability of totalSupply being exactly 0 at epoch boundaries in a real deployment with active stakers
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test notifyRewardAmount with varying _remainingRewardTime values and totalSupply=0 to determine reward loss proportions
Test the interaction between _updateRewardsForUser ordering and balance updates to confirm first-staker reward skipping
Test if calling notifyRewardAmount again after the lost period can partially recover through the carry-over mechanism (it should not be possible since periodFinish has passed)
